meta.title: Church and State — a register of the UK's closed and at-risk churches
meta.note: Schema v2 — nested by editorial panel. St Tyfrydog's is the strawman, fully sourced across every slot. Other buildings are partial; empty slots are honest nulls, not hidden gaps.

buildings.name: St Tyfrydog's
buildings.summary: A tiny medieval church in a circular churchyard, half-reclaimed by ivy. Closed in 2020 after congregations had fallen to three or four people. Vested in Friends of Friendless Churches this year; a new roof and drainage are planned for 2026 at an estimated cost of £350,000.

listing.reason: A good Medieval rural church which retains much of its Medieval fabric.

associated.note: Brass, 18th century, standing in the base of a medieval stone cross

phases.what: Nave and chancel arch — the oldest surviving parts of the present building
First major restoration; box pews added
Second restoration; porch and vestry added

fabric.materials: Rough, small, squared stones, dressed with limestone. Slate roof with a stone bellcote.
fabric.plan: Nave c. 24'9" × 23'6"; chancel c. 23'3" × 18'9"; pointed arch between.

features: Painted Georgian box pews with panelled doors and open back benches — 19th-century restoration
Pale blue plaster between the rafters, against dark arched-braced collared roof trusses
Three-light east window of late-15th-century stonework, glazed 1873 by W.H. Sullivan of Liverpool — the only stained glass
Single bell in the stone bellcote (clapper rusted and fallen)
Elizabethan silver communion cup and a silver paten dated 1721

statusHistory.event: Last service held; church closed for worship
Vested in Friends of Friendless Churches

condition.summary: Roof leaking in places, daylight visible through it. Ivy climbing the exterior walls and advancing into the porch. Plaster fallen from the ceiling onto the box pews. Vestry floor collapsed under the weight of a table. Bell clapper rusted and fallen.
plannedWorks: Patching the holes in the roof

context.ruralUrban: Smaller rural: Further from a major town or city (ONS 2021)
context.ruralUrbanPrior: Rural hamlet and isolated dwellings in a sparse setting (2011)

use.servicesStatus: None — closed for worship since November 2020
use.lateCongregationSize: Three to six regulars; larger for Christmas and harvest festival

people.name: Jane Bown
people.role: Wife of the former warden; played the organ at services

people.name: Peggy Thomas
people.role: Tom's sister; married at the church 21 July 1962

people.name: The Rev Kevin Ellis
people.role: Took the last service on 22 November 2020

quotes.quote: What upset Tom most when it closed was that the key went, and he was never allowed into the church again.
You can't carry on if people aren't going; there's no point sending clergy if there's no one there.

If we didn't step in, what would happen here? It would just sit there, the holes in the roof would get bigger, the ivy would take over, it would still be a problem in 10 years' time — just a bigger problem.
quotes.attribution: Rachel Morley, Friends of Friendless Churches

guardian.title: The Welsh church claimed by spiders and ivy: what do Britain's derelict churches say about our health and happiness?
guardian.author: Sam Wollaston

sources.label: Friends of Friendless Churches — St Tyfrydog's

buildings.provenance: Identity, fabric and interior from the Cadw list entry, RCAHMW's Coflein record, the Friends of Friendless Churches page and Wikipedia. Geography from postcodes.io (LL71 8AG). Photograph Nigel Williams via Geograph, CC-BY-SA 2.0. People, quotes and dispatch from The Guardian, 23 April 2026. WIMD decile still to source.

buildings.name: St Baglan's
buildings.summary: A small stone church set alone in a field above the Menai Strait, filled with 18th-century box pews and lit only by candles. No electricity, no road to the door — reached on foot across the fields.

features: Lit only by candles — no electricity

statusHistory.event: Vested in Friends of Friendless Churches

condition.summary: Sound following conservation works. Used occasionally for services and events.

use.openingHours: By arrangement; reached on foot across the fields

buildings.provenance: Sparse schema-v2 record. Coordinate from OSM. Everything else pending Cadw / Coflein / FoFC direct fetch.

buildings.name: All Saints
buildings.summary: A medieval parish church in a Holderness village of around 2,000 people. The congregation has been unable to raise the £250,000 needed to repair the roof — a sum the National Churches Trust notes would probably have been reachable in a wealthier part of the country.

condition.summary: Roof repairs required. The congregation has been unable to raise the £250,000 needed.

quotes.quote: Even with declining attendances, if that was Hertfordshire, the community would probably have been able to rustle up the money.
quotes.attribution: Sir Philip Rutnam, National Churches Trust

buildings.provenance: Sparse schema-v2 record. Named in The Guardian, 23 April 2026. Listing, fabric and condition detail pending Historic England NHLE + Heritage at Risk fetch.

buildings.name: St Mary's
buildings.summary: A lonely church among oak trees and saltmarsh on the Essex coast, famous for its weatherboarded timber belfry and 18th-century box pews. One of Friends of Friendless Churches' earliest rescues — without which it would almost certainly have been demolished.

fabric.materials: Stone with a weatherboarded timber-framed bell-tower.

statusHistory.event: Declared redundant and vested in Friends of Friendless Churches

condition.summary: Conserved. Opened by appointment and for occasional services.

buildings.provenance: Sparse schema-v2 record. Coordinate from OSM.

buildings.name: Brick Lane Jamme Masjid
buildings.summary: One building, four congregations. A Huguenot church in 1743; a Wesleyan Methodist chapel in 1819; the Machzike Adass synagogue from 1898; a mosque since 1976. A rare example of a place of worship that has passed from one community to the next as the streets around it changed.

phases.what: Built as La Neuve Église for French Huguenot refugees
Re-consecrated as the Machzike Adass synagogue

condition.summary: In active use as a mosque.

buildings.provenance: Sparse schema-v2 record. Coordinate from OSM.

buildings.name: Abney Park Chapel
buildings.summary: A roofless Gothic chapel at the heart of the UK's first purpose-built non-denominational burial ground, and later a magnificent Victorian cemetery. Stripped by fire and decay since the 1970s; partial restoration begun but the interior remains open to the sky.

phases.what: Built by William Hosking — Britain's first purpose-built non-denominational chapel

custodian.body: Abney Park Trust / London Borough of Hackney

condition.summary: Roofless. Stonework stabilised. Partial restoration completed 2023; interior remains open to the sky.

buildings.provenance: Sparse schema-v2 record. Coordinate is Abney Park cemetery centroid.
